sections.title: Use
items: Actions or states that took place before a specific point in the past: By the time the guests arrived, we **had already set** the table. She **had worked** at the company for ten years before she became manager.
Past actions whose results were relevant at a particular moment in the past: The players were celebrating because they **had won** the match.

sections.title: Useful notes
rows: I'd finished my homework **by** eight o'clock.
**By the time** I got to class, the lesson had started.
The teacher had checked the answers **before** the lesson.
I left **after** I'd finished the test.
Simon had **just** finished the test when the bell rang.
I left **when** I'd finished the test.

sections.title: Be careful
items: Choosing between the past simple and the past perfect simple changes the order of events.
The show started when we arrived. (= We arrived first, then the show started.)
The show had started when we arrived. (= The show started first, then we arrived.)
Some verbs have irregular past participle forms.

sections.title: Use
items: Activities that were in progress up to, or until just before, a moment in the past: He was out of breath because he **had been running**. They deserved a rest — they **had been working** all morning.

sections.title: Useful notes
rows: Tony had been studying **for** hours, so he had a headache.
She'd been hoping to win the competition **since** the summer.
We'd been talking about the Internet **before** the lesson started.

sections.title: Be careful
items: The past perfect simple often emphasises the result of an action: ✓ She'd **written** an article for the school newspaper. (= She'd finished it.)

The past perfect continuous often emphasises the action, and the time spent on the action, rather than the result: ✓ She'd **been writing** an article for the newspaper. (= She'd started, but she hadn't finished it.)

exercises.title: A: Past perfect simple
exercises.instructions: Complete using the correct past perfect simple form of the verbs in brackets.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using the correct past perfect simple form of the verbs in brackets.

items.sentence: When we got to the station, the train ...... (already / leave).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is the train left before we arrived, so the correct answer is "had already left".

wrongOptions.already left: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use past perfect when one past action happened before another.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect describes an action that happened before another past action.

items.sentence: Mia ...... (finish) her homework before dinner.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is homework finished before dinner, so the correct answer is "had finished".

wrongOptions.finished: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had finished", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They ...... (not / book) a table, so the restaurant was full.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no table booked before they arrived, so the correct answer is "hadn't booked".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use past perfect when one past action happened before another.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect describes an action that happened before another past action.

items.sentence: ...... (Tom / ever / fly) before he went to Canada?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is experience before Canada, so the correct answer is "Had Tom ever flown".

wrongOptions.tom ever flown: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The match started after ...... (the referee / check) the pitch.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is referee checked before the match started, so the correct answer is "the referee had checked".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "the referee had checked", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: I couldn't buy a ticket because ...... (I / leave) my wallet at home.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wallet left at home before trying to buy, so the correct answer is "I had left".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "I had left", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: ...... (you / already / finish) lunch when I arrived?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lunch finished before I arrived, so the correct answer is "Had you already finished".

wrongOptions.you already finished: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use past perfect when one past action happened before another.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect describes an action that happened before another past action.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Choose the sentence with the same meaning
exercises.title: B: Choose the sentence with the same meaning
exercises.instructions: Choose the option that means the same.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the option that means the same.

items.context: We had cleaned the kitchen when Mum came home.
items.sentenceBefore: We had cleaned the kitchen when Mum came home.
options: Mum came home and then we cleaned the kitchen.
We cleaned the kitchen and then Mum came home.
items.originalSentence: We had cleaned the kitchen when Mum came home.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice uses a form of be + past participle. Match the tense of be to the time clue (each year → present, yesterday → past, etc.).

wrongOptions.Mum came home and then we cleaned the ki: "Mum came home and then we cleaned the kitchen." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for by + agent and time clues — they tell you which passive tense fits.

optionContrast.Mum came home and then we cleaned the ki: "Mum came home and then we cleaned the kitchen." doesn't fit here → "B" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
optionContrast.We cleaned the kitchen and then Mum came: "We cleaned the kitchen and then Mum came home." doesn't fit here → "B" does, because Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.context: I called Sam after I had finished my project.
items.sentenceBefore: I called Sam after I had finished my project.
options: I finished my project and then I called Sam.
I called Sam and then I finished my project.
items.originalSentence: I called Sam after I had finished my project.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.I finished my project and then I called: "I finished my project and then I called Sam." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.I finished my project and then I called: "I finished my project and then I called Sam." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
optionContrast.I called Sam and then I finished my proj: "I called Sam and then I finished my project." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.context: By the time the film started, we'd bought popcorn.
items.sentenceBefore: By the time the film started, we'd bought popcorn.
options: We bought popcorn before the film started.
We bought popcorn after the film started.
items.originalSentence: By the time the film started, we'd bought popcorn.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

wrongOptions.We bought popcorn before the film starte: "We bought popcorn before the film started." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

optionContrast.We bought popcorn before the film starte: "We bought popcorn before the film started." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
optionContrast.We bought popcorn after the film started: "We bought popcorn after the film started." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.context: Lily didn't leave until her brother had arrived.
items.sentenceBefore: Lily didn't leave until her brother had arrived.
options: Lily left before her brother arrived.
Her brother arrived and then Lily left.
items.originalSentence: Lily didn't leave until her brother had arrived.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

"Lily left before her brother arrived." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

"Lily left before her brother arrived." doesn't fit here → "B" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
"Her brother arrived and then Lily left." doesn't fit here → "B" does, because Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.context: The teacher hadn't marked the tests by Friday.
items.sentenceBefore: The teacher hadn't marked the tests by Friday.
options: The tests were still unmarked on Friday.
The teacher marked the tests before Friday.
items.originalSentence: The teacher hadn't marked the tests by Friday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

"The tests were still unmarked on Friday." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

"The tests were still unmarked on Friday." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
optionContrast.The teacher marked the tests before Frid: "The teacher marked the tests before Friday." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.context: They had sold the bike before I phoned about it.
items.sentenceBefore: They had sold the bike before I phoned about it.
options: They sold the bike and later I phoned.
I phoned and then they sold the bike.
items.originalSentence: They had sold the bike before I phoned about it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

"They sold the bike and later I phoned." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

"They sold the bike and later I phoned." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
"I phoned and then they sold the bike." doesn't fit here → "A" does, because Only "A" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

items.context: Dad had cooked dinner when we arrived.
items.sentenceBefore: Dad had cooked dinner when we arrived.
options: We arrived and then Dad cooked dinner.
Dad cooked dinner and then we arrived.
items.originalSentence: Dad had cooked dinner when we arrived.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple describes habits, routines and general facts. Present continuous describes actions happening now or temporary situations.

"We arrived and then Dad cooked dinner." does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Look for time clues — frequency words (very often, usually) point to present simple; right now and at the moment point to present continuous.

"We arrived and then Dad cooked dinner." doesn't fit here → "B" does, because it does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence.
"Dad cooked dinner and then we arrived." doesn't fit here → "B" does, because Only "B" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Write sentences using the prompts
exercises.title: C: Write sentences using the prompts
exercises.instructions: Write sentences using the prompts. One of the verbs must be in the past perfect simple.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write sentences using the prompts. One of the verbs must be in the past perfect simple.

items.displayPrompt: they / already / leave / when / we / arrive
items.answer: They had already left when we arrived.
acceptedAnswers: They had already left when we arrived.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: They left before we arrived, so the sentence is "They had already left when we arrived.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: they / already / leave / when / we / arrive → They had already left when we arrived.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.already: "already" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.leave: Conjugate "leave" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "leave" in the answer.
wrongOptions.when: "when" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.arrive: Conjugate "arrive" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "arrive" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "leave" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.displayPrompt: I / not / meet / Anna / before / she / join / our class
items.answer: I hadn't met Anna before she joined our class.
acceptedAnswers: I hadn't met Anna before she joined our class.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Meeting Anna happened before she joined, so the sentence is "I hadn't met Anna before she joined our class.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: I / not / meet / Anna / before / she / join / our class → I hadn't met Anna before she joined our class.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.not: "not" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.meet: Conjugate "meet" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "meet" in the answer.
wrongOptions.before: "before" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.join: Conjugate "join" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "join" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "meet" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.displayPrompt: when / Dad / get home / we / already / eat / dinner
items.answer: When Dad got home, we had already eaten dinner.
acceptedAnswers: When Dad got home, we had already eaten dinner.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Dinner eaten before Dad got home, so the sentence is "When Dad got home, we had already eaten dinner.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: when / Dad / get home / we / already / eat / dinner → When Dad got home, we had already eaten dinner.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.when: "when" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.get home: Conjugate "get home" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "get home" in the answer.
wrongOptions.already: "already" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.eat: Conjugate "eat" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "eat" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "get home" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.displayPrompt: he / be / tired / because / he / not / sleep / well
items.answer: He was tired because he hadn't slept well.
acceptedAnswers: He was tired because he hadn't slept well.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Bad sleep before feeling tired, so the sentence is "He was tired because he hadn't slept well.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: he / be / tired / because / he / not / sleep / well → He was tired because he hadn't slept well.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.be: Conjugate "be" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "be" in the answer.
wrongOptions.because: "because" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.not: "not" is a connector or modifier — place it in the correct position in the sentence.
wrongOptions.sleep: Conjugate "sleep" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "sleep" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "be" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

items.displayPrompt: by the time / the concert / begin / everyone / find / their seats
items.answer: By the time the concert began, everyone had found their seats.
acceptedAnswers: By the time the concert began, everyone had found their seats.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Seats found before the concert began, so the sentence is "By the time the concert began, everyone had found their seats.".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Cues: by the time / the concert / begin / everyone / find / their seats → By the time the concert began, everyone had found their seats.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect shows the earlier of two past actions — use had + past participle before the later past event.

wrongOptions.begin: Conjugate "begin" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "begin" in the answer.
wrongOptions.find: Conjugate "find" to fit the tense and subject — it does not stay as "find" in the answer.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Conjugate "begin" correctly — verb form is the most common error with these cues.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Start with the subject, then place time words and connectors before conjugating the main verb.

exercises.title: D: Past perfect continuous
exercises.instructions: Look at the pictures and complete the sentences. Use the correct form of the past perfect continuous.
exercises.studentInstruction: Look at the pictures and complete the sentences. Use the correct form of the past perfect continuous.

items.sentence: He was covered in paint because ...... (paint / the fence).
items.answer: he had been painting the fence
acceptedAnswers: he had been painting the fence

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is paint on him shows ongoing activity before now, so the correct answer is "he had been painting the fence".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "he had been painting the fence", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: They were exhausted because ...... (walk / all morning).
items.answer: they had been walking all morning
acceptedAnswers: they had been walking all morning

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is exhaustion from walking all morning, so the correct answer is "they had been walking all morning".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "they had been walking all morning", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: The streets were wet because ...... (it / rain).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wet streets show rain before, so the correct answer is "it had been raining".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "it had been raining", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Did she lose her voice because ...... (sing / for hours)?
items.answer: she had been singing for hours
acceptedAnswers: she had been singing for hours

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is singing for hours caused the lost voice, so the correct answer is "she had been singing for hours".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "she had been singing for hours", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: When the bus came, ...... (we / wait / for forty minutes).
items.answer: we had been waiting for forty minutes
acceptedAnswers: we had been waiting for forty minutes

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is waiting started before the bus came, so the correct answer is "we had been waiting for forty minutes".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "we had been waiting for forty minutes", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: When I called, ...... (they / not / study / long).

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is they hadn't studied long before the call, so the correct answer is "they hadn't been studying long".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "they hadn't been studying long", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentenceAfter: for ten minutes when the phone rang.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "been sleeping" — only ten minutes of sleep before the phone rang.

wrongOptions.A: "slept" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "been sleeping" is correct.
wrongOptions.slept: "slept" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "been sleeping" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "been sleeping" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

optionContrast.A: "slept" doesn't fit here → "been sleeping" does, because it does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "been sleeping" is correct.
optionContrast.slept: "slept" doesn't fit here → "been sleeping" does, because it does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "been sleeping" is correct.
optionContrast.been sleeping: "been sleeping" doesn't fit here → "been sleeping" does, because The context points to "been sleeping" — only ten minutes of sleep before the phone rang.

wrongOptions.B: "been buying" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "bought" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

optionContrast.bought: "bought" doesn't fit here → "bought" does, because The context points to "bought" — tickets bought before you called.
optionContrast.B: "been buying" doesn't fit here → "bought" does, because it does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "bought" is correct.
optionContrast.been buying: "been buying" doesn't fit here → "bought" does, because it does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "bought" is correct.

wrongOptions.B: "been going" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "been" is correct.

optionContrast.been: "been" doesn't fit here → "been" does, because The context points to "been" — never been abroad before this trip.
optionContrast.B: "been going" doesn't fit here → "been" does, because the sentence describes a general habit, not a temporary action.
optionContrast.been going: "been going" doesn't fit here → "been" does, because the sentence describes a general habit, not a temporary action.

exercises.title: F: Find the extra word
exercises.instructions: If a line is correct, click OK. If there is an extra word in a line, click on it.
exercises.studentInstruction: If the line is correct, tap OK. If there is an extra word, tap it.

items.sentence: Yesterday our team finally [had] won the school basketball final.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Yesterday needs past simple won — had is the extra word.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Read the sentence aloud without the tapped word — if it sounds natural, that word is the extra one.

wrongOptions.Yesterday: "Yesterday" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.our: "our" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.team: "team" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.finally: "finally" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.had: "had" is the redundant word — tap it (do not tap OK).
wrongOptions.won: "won" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.the: "the" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.school: "school" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.basketball: "basketball" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.final: "final" is necessary here — the extra word is "had".
wrongOptions.OK: There is an extra word ("had") — do not tap OK.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Look for a repeated or unnecessary small word — "had" is the one to tap, not a content word the sentence needs.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Try reading the sentence without each word in your head — the extra word is the one that can be dropped with no loss of meaning.

items.sentence: We had [been] trained hard for weeks, so everyone was excited.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Had been trained is wrong here; been is the extra word — say had trained.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present simple passive uses is/are + past participle — been is not needed after is in this pattern.
